title: 4.1 and 4.2 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lessons 4.1 and 4.2!
topic_path: Empires and Belief Systems (200 to 1500 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did portable belief systems differ from earlier, more localized belief systems?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They were not tied to a specific place and could spread across large regions.
They relied entirely on oral tradition and avoided written texts.
They rejected all forms of religious rituals and traditions.
They were limited to elite classes and avoided mass conversion.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does it mean when a religion is described as “universal”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Anyone willing to adopt the religion’s core beliefs and practices can become a member.
Anyone can join if they move to a place of worship.
It means the religion’s origins are mysterious and not fully known.
It means religion can be used by governments to shape behavior and control people.
Animism recognizes a spiritual world, which is related to the natural world.
Animism recognizes spirits as “amiable”, or gentle, forces in the world.
Animism recognizes a singular, main spirit that controls the natural world.
Animism recognizes animals as the connection between the spiritual and the natural world.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What did Confucius argue had to be done for order to be restored in society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Societies had to promote learning and virtues.
Societies had to reward virtuous people.
Societies had to punish disobedient people.
Societies had to separate “superior” and “inferior” people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how Confucianism used family roles and the "Five Key Relationships” to structure society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It promoted a strict social order where each person had a defined role and responsibility based on their position.
It rejected hierarchy and encouraged equality among all family and community members.
It encouraged people to isolate themselves from their families to focus on personal enlightenment.
It required everyone to participate in government service regardless of their status.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how Confucianism differs from many other major belief systems?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Rather than focus on gods or the afterlife, it emphasizes social harmony and ethical behavior.
It encourages withdrawal from society to seek enlightenment through meditation.
It promotes monotheistic belief in a single, all-powerful deity.
It relies heavily on a written set of laws to govern moral behavior.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how Buddhism challenged traditional Vedic beliefs in ancient India?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It rejected the caste system and emphasized individual paths to enlightenment.
It promoted the authority of Brahmins as spiritual leaders.
It introduced the worship of multiple gods and elaborate rituals.
It focused on achieving success through material wealth and social status.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains why Buddhism was able to spread beyond its place of origin?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Its teachings were easily adaptable to different cultures and its message was universal.
It forced conversion through military conquest and imperial rule.
It emphasized strict monastic isolation, limiting interaction with broader society.
Its emphasis on obedience to authority made it especially appealing to imperial rulers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Buddhism change as it spread into China?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It absorbed ideas such as ancestor veneration and respect for elders.
It eliminated opportunities for women by reinforcing strict gender hierarchies.
It struggled to gain acceptance in China, where Confucianism and Daoism were firmly established.
It adopted core concepts like the Four Noble Truths from Chinese belief systems.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the relationship between Judaism and Christianity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Christianity developed from Jewish beliefs, but introduced new teachings centered on Jesus as the Messiah.
Judaism and Christianity are identical in beliefs but differ only in language and cultural practices.
Christianity was formed to reject all Jewish teachings and created an entirely new religious tradition.
Judaism was formed to preserve ancient myths after the rise of Christianity.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did Roman authorities view Christianity as a threat to their society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Christians refusal to worship Roman gods or the emperor challenged loyalty to the state.
Christians demanded political control and sought to overthrow the Roman government.
Christianity supported the Roman class system and reinforced imperial authority.
Christians rejected all forms of written law, including Roman legal traditions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the role of women in early Christianity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Jesus viewed men and women as equal, but later church leaders restricted women’s roles.
Jesus taught that women were less worthy of salvation than men, shaping early Christian doctrine.
Jesus promoted full equality between men and women, and the early Church upheld this teaching.
Jesus believed women were spiritually superior, though church leaders later restricted their roles.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Now therefore, if you will indeed obey My voice and keep My covenant, you shall be My treasured possession among all peoples... and you shall be to Me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.  
—Exodus 19:5–6 (Tanakh)


**Based on the passage above, which of the following best reflects a core belief of ancient Judaism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Jewish people were chosen by God and had a special responsibility to uphold His laws.
Ancient Jewish society was governed entirely by elected leaders and democratic councils.
The main purpose of Jewish life was to conquer neighboring lands and spread their religion.
Salvation could be earned through personal wealth and temple offerings.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the Jewish belief in the Covenant?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A sacred agreement where the Jewish people promised to follow God’s laws in return for His protection.
The belief that the Hebrews would become God's chosen people only by settling in the “holy land.”
A political alliance formed by ancient Jewish leaders to expand their territory.
A belief that faith alone guarantees salvation, without the need to follow religious laws.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the role of karma and dharma in the Hindu belief system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They guided moral behavior and influenced how a person would be reborn in their next life.
They were rules created by kings to maintain order in Hindu society.
They taught that salvation could only be achieved by withdrawing from all worldly activity.
They required strict worship of a single god through daily temple rituals.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was the social hierarchy structured under Hinduism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The caste system organized people based on their social and religious roles in society.
The caste system was based primarily on skin color, separating lighter and darker groups.
The caste system ranked people according to how they died and their funeral practices.
The caste system grouped people based on how much they produced or contributed economically.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What made Hinduism appealing to many people?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The belief that good behavior in this life could lead to a better rebirth in the next.
The comfort of knowing that salvation was automatic and did not need to be earned.
The idea that people did not need contact with the gods for moral or spiritual guidance.
The promise that all people were treated equally, regardless of social class.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How are Islam, Judaism, and Christianity similar?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: All three believe in one God and share some of the same prophets.
All three require worship of multiple gods for different aspects of life.
All three reject the use of sacred texts and rely only on oral traditions.
All three began in East Asia and emphasize reincarnation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the *ummah* (Muslim community) split early in Islam’s history?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Disagreement over who should lead after Muhammad’s death caused a division between Sunni and Shi’a Muslims.
Disagreement over interpretations of the Qur’an split the *ummah* into Sunni and Shi’a Muslims
The ummah split naturally when Islamic expansion slowed and believers became isolated across regions.
The rise of Muslim empires led to different versions of Islamic law, causing the early division.
